headline: Steamboats Reach Fort Benton, 1860
body:
The great Missouri, that untamed serpent of the plains, had long defied the ambitions of men. Its currents, shifting and treacherous, its snags and sandbars, were a constant challenge to those who sought to bind the vast interior to the arteries of commerce. Yet, on the second day of July, in the year of our Lord 1860, two vessels of iron and steam, the *Chippewa* and the *Key West*, did at last conquer its final, most formidable stretch. They nosed their way to the muddy banks of Fort Benton, a settlement then but a raw outpost, yet destined to become the very navel of the continent's inland navigation.

This was no mere journey; it was a declaration. For years, the American Fur Company, with its keen eye for profit and its unyielding will, had pushed the limits of river travel. The *Chippewa* herself, a vessel of lighter draft, had in the previous year, 1859, come within a mere fifteen miles of this very spot, discharging her precious cargo at Brulé bottom. It was a tantalizing whisper of what was to come, a promise of dominion over the wild waters. The men who piloted these craft were a breed apart, their eyes sharp, their hands steady, their knowledge of the river's moods encyclopedic. Of Captain John La Barge, a man whose very name was synonymous with the Missouri, the *Sioux City Eagle* had, on July 23, 1859, declared with an almost prophetic certainty: "Captain John La Barge, one of the oldest and best steam boat men on the river, takes command of the Chippewa, and if the trip to Fort Benton can be made, he will make it!" Such was the faith placed in these river-hardened individuals.

Their arrival transformed Fort Benton from a remote trading post, a mere speck on the map of the wilderness, into a bustling entrepôt. It became, in essence, the world's innermost port, a vital nexus where the raw materials of the mountains met the manufactured goods of the East. The period from 1855 to 1860 was, as the chroniclers would later note, the golden era of steamboating on the Missouri, a brief, glorious interlude before the iron horse of the railroad would claim its supremacy. But for this fleeting moment, the steamboat reigned supreme, its paddlewheels churning a path for civilization, for prospectors lured by the glint of gold, for settlers seeking new lives, and for the endless flow of supplies that fueled the westward expansion. The river, once a barrier, became a highway, and Fort Benton, perched at its farthest navigable reach, stood as a testament to human ingenuity and the relentless march of progress.
